pattern: A bitonic array has exactly one peak, so locating it once and summing the two overlapping halves it defines is all that is ever needed to compare them.
hints: Because the array strictly rises to a single peak and then strictly falls, that peak is just the maximum element -- and it belongs to both halves at once.
Find the index of the maximum value, sum everything from the start through that index for the ascending part, and sum everything from that index through the end for the descending part.
Compare the two sums directly: 0 if ascending is bigger, 1 if descending is bigger, and -1 only when they come out exactly equal.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, the two parts tie exactly
the peak is at the very first index (a strictly decreasing array), the ascending part is just the peak alone
the peak is at the very last index (a strictly increasing array), the descending part is just the peak alone
the smallest possible bitonic array, exactly 3 elements
a longer array where the two sides sum to exactly the same total, not just the minimal 4-element case
values near the maximum bound, checking the comparison holds up at scale

stress.description: random bitonic arrays built by choosing a peak position and filling each side with distinct random values below it
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(3, min(size, 100000))
    a = rng.randint(1, n)
    b = n - a + 1
    peak = 1000000000
    pool_needed = (a - 1) + (b - 1)
    chosen = set()
    while len(chosen) < pool_needed:
        chosen.add(rng.randint(1, peak - 1))
    chosen = list(chosen)
    rng.shuffle(chosen)
    asc_vals = sorted(chosen[:a - 1])
    desc_vals = sorted(chosen[a - 1:], reverse=True)
    nums = asc_vals + [peak] + desc_vals
    return [nums]
